tidyfault 0.0.0.9
  • Reference

    quantify_prob() Function

    quantify_prob.Rd

    Computes the top event failure probability from a fault tree when given basic event failure probabilities. Assumes independent basic events; uses the full truth table to sum probabilities over all failure combinations.

    quantify_prob(f, newdata, truth_table = NULL)

    Arguments

    f

    (Required) Function outputted by formulate(), with one argument per basic event. Used to identify event names and, if truth_table is not supplied, to generate the truth table via calculate(f).

    newdata

    (Required) Failure probabilities (0 to 1) for each basic event. Can be a single vector or list (one scenario), or a data frame or matrix with one row per scenario and columns for each basic event (names must match formalArgs(f)). Unnamed vectors are interpreted in formalArgs(f) order. Values should be in [0, 1], where each value represents the probability that the corresponding basic event occurs.

    truth_table

    (Optional) Data frame with one column per basic event (0/1) and an outcome column (1 = system failure, 0 = no failure). If provided, the truth table is not recomputed (useful when calculate() was already run).

    Value

    A numeric vector of top-event failure probabilities. If newdata is a single scenario (vector/list or single-row data frame), returns a single numeric value. If newdata has multiple rows (one per scenario), returns a numeric vector with one probability per scenario (same order as rows of newdata). Similar to R's predict() function style.

    Details

    The top event probability is computed exactly via the complete truth table: for each of the 2^n combinations of basic event states, the probability of that combination is the product of newdata[e] for events that occur (1) and 1 - newdata[e] for events that do not (0). The function sums these probabilities over all combinations where outcome == 1. This is exact for independent basic events but has complexity O(2^n); for trees with many basic events, consider using minimal cut set approximations elsewhere. When newdata has multiple rows, the truth table is computed once and all scenarios are evaluated in one pass.

    See also

    formulate for creating the function from a boolean equation, calculate for generating the truth table, quantify for evaluating whether the top event occurs given binary (0/1) basic event states. The result matches the FaultTree package when the same tree is built and ftree.calc(DF, use.bdd = TRUE) is used (BDD accounts for repeated basic events; gate-by-gate with use.bdd = FALSE does not).

    Examples

    library(tidyverse)
library(tidyfault)
data("fakenodes")
data("fakeedges")

f <- curate(nodes = fakenodes, edges = fakeedges) %>%
  equate() %>%
  formulate()
# Pipe-friendly: probability vector in event order (e.g. A, B, C, D)
curate(nodes = fakenodes, edges = fakeedges) %>%
  equate() %>%
  formulate() %>%
  quantify_prob(c(0.1, 0.2, 0.05, 0.15))
#> [1] 0.01285

# Named probs (names must match event names in the tree)
probs <- setNames(c(0.1, 0.2, 0.05, 0.15), formalArgs(f))
f %>% quantify_prob(probs)
#> [1] 0.01285

# Reuse an existing truth table to avoid recomputing
tt <- calculate(f)
f %>% quantify_prob(probs, truth_table = tt)
#> [1] 0.01285

# Many scenarios at once (vectorized): pass a data frame, one row per scenario
probs_df <- as.data.frame(replicate(4, runif(1000), simplify = FALSE))
names(probs_df) <- formalArgs(f)
f %>% quantify_prob(probs_df, truth_table = tt)  # length-1000 vector
#>    [1] 0.2178997507 0.2617878853 0.3344412228 0.1459554111 0.6825921344
#>    [6] 0.3328664387 0.0751405386 0.6855530884 0.3512877529 0.6722321687
